title: Precision, Recall, and F1-Score
concept: Precision measures quality of positive predictions, Recall measures quantity of positive cases detected, and F1-score balances both.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary weakness of simple accuracy as a metric?

options.text: It fails to reflect performance on rare classes in imbalanced data.
It is computationally slow to calculate.
It cannot be used with cross-entropy loss.

exercises.explain: Correct! Accuracy is easily inflated by majority classes, ignoring rare but critical categories.

cards.title: Fishing with a net vs. a spear
cards.body: If you cast a giant net into the ocean, you catch many fish, but also seaweed, boots, and plastic (high Recall, low Precision). If you use a spear, you catch only the exact fish you target, but you miss many others swimming by (high Precision, low Recall).

cards.title: Key Metrics
cards.body:
Precision: TP / (TP + FP). Fraction of predicted positives that are actually positive.
Recall: TP / (TP + FN). Fraction of actual positives that the model successfully found.
F1-Score: Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, balancing both.

cards.title: Buying grocery items
cards.body: Think of buying organic apples. Precision is making sure every apple in your bag is actually organic (no non-organic snuck in). Recall is making sure you bought every organic apple available in the store.

cards.title: Spam filter metrics
cards.body: A spam filter must have high Precision: if it predicts 'Spam' (positive) for an important business email, it deletes it. We accept lower Recall (some spam slips into the inbox) to prevent deleting important mail.

cards.title: The tradeoff is unavoidable
cards.body: You might think a perfect model can achieve 100% precision and 100% recall. In practice, they exist in tension: to increase recall (catch more items), you must broaden your search, which inevitably sweeps in false positives, lowering precision.

left.text:
Formula: TP / Predicted Positives
Checks: Are our alarms correct?
Penalizes False Positives (FP)

right.text:
Formula: TP / Actual Positives
Checks: Did we miss anything?
Penalizes False Negatives (FN)

exercises.prompt: If a model predicts 10 emails are spam, but 3 of them are actually clean work emails, what is the Precision of the model?
exercises.explain: Precision is correct positive predictions (7) divided by all predicted positive predictions (10), which is 0.70.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The F1-Score is calculated as a simple arithmetic average of Precision and Recall.
exercises.explain: False. F1-Score is the harmonic mean: 2 * (Precision * Recall) / (Precision + Recall), which penalizes extreme imbalances between the two.
False negatives are true items that the model missed (predicted negative), which lowers recall.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario requires a model prioritized for high Recall over Precision?
exercises.explain: Medical diagnostics prioritize high recall to ensure every potential case is caught for doctor review.

options.text: A cancer screening model where missing a tumor (False Negatives) is unacceptable
A court sentencing model where sentencing an innocent person (False Positives) is unacceptable
An email system where placing clean mail in spam is unacceptable

exercises.prompt: A model detects shoplifters in drone feeds. Out of 100 actual shoplifting events, the model catches 80. However, the model also incorrectly flags 20 innocent shoppers as shoplifters. What are the Recall and Precision?

options.text: Recall = 0.80 (80 of 100 caught); Precision = 0.80 (80 correct out of 100 total flagged).

exercises.explain: Exactly! The model found 80 out of 100 true cases (Recall = 80/100 = 0.80). It made 100 positive calls in total (80 true + 20 false flags), so 80 of those 100 were correct (Precision = 80/100 = 0.80). Next, let's look at how we map these values in a grid!
